title: Scaphoid Fracture
oneLiner: Common wrist fracture in young adults after fall on outstretched hand; high risk of avascular necrosis (AVN) and non-union if missed or undertreated.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Acute carpal tunnel syndrome:** Median nerve compression from fracture haematoma — *key finding: thenar weakness, loss of two-point discrimination (<6 mm) or severe paraesthesia*. Needs urgent surgical decompression.
- **Compartment syndrome of forearm/wrist:** Rare but catastrophic. *Key finding: pain on passive extension of fingers out of proportion to injury + tense swelling*. Check compartment pressure if suspected.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
    - Fall on outstretched hand (FOOSH) — typically young adult (15–40 y)
    - Radial-sided wrist pain, worse with grip and thumb movements
    - Snuffbox tenderness (anatomical snuffbox — *most sensitive sign*)
    - Tenderness over scaphoid tubercle (volar wrist, just distal to distal wrist crease)
    - Pain on axial loading of thumb (compression test)
    - Reduced range of wrist motion (especially extension)
- **Key exam findings:**
    - Swelling may be minimal — pain out of proportion to swelling
    - Pain on scaphoid compression: thumb metacarpal pushed proximally
    - No deformity in most cases (unless displaced)
    - Check distal neurovascular status: median, ulnar, radial nerves
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Scaphoid fracture (most likely) —** snuffbox tenderness, pain on axial compression
- **Distal radius fracture —** obvious deformity, radial styloid tenderness, dinner fork deformity (Colles’)
- **Wrist sprain / ligament injury —** no point tenderness over scaphoid, full motion less painful
- **De Quervain’s tenosynovitis —** Finkelstein test positive, no FOOSH history
- **Thumb metacarpal / trapezium fracture —** pain more ulnar or at base of thumb
- **Carpal instability (scapholunate dissociation) —** positive Watson’s test, often associated with history of hyperextension
- **Splitter:** *Exquisite tenderness precisely in anatomical snuffbox + pain on thumb axial compression = scaphoid until proven otherwise; if both absent and X-ray negative, think sprain*
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
    - Clinical examination as above — snuffbox tenderness has sensitivity ~90% for acute fracture
    - Assess for median nerve function (thumb abduction, sensation to pulp of index)
    - Check radial pulse and capillary refill
- **Bloods:**
    - Typically not required unless planned for surgery (FBC, UAE, coagulation)
- **Imaging:**
    - **First-line:** Plain X-ray wrist — scaphoid view (PA in ulnar deviation) + lateral + oblique views. *If initial X-ray negative but clinical suspicion high → treat as fracture*.
    - **Second-line (if high suspicion after 10–14 days of negative X-ray):** MRI wrist (gold standard, sensitivity >99%) or CT if MRI unavailable. *Don’t delay treatment waiting for imaging.*
- **Special tests:**
    - **Scaphoid compression test** (axial load on thumb) — positive if pain at scaphoid
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**
    - No validated score in routine use; diagnosis is clinical + imaging
- **Never-miss test:** *MRI wrist within 10–14 days if initial X-ray negative and clinical suspicion high.* Missing an occult scaphoid fracture leads to AVN.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
    - Immobilise in scaphoid cast or below-elbow backslab (thumb immobilised — position: wrist neutral, thumb opposed). Simple volar wrist splint acceptable if cast unavailable.
    - Analgesia: paracetamol 1g PO QDS + ibuprofen 400mg PO TDS (if no contraindication)
    - Ice pack, elevation, simple sling
    - Refer to orthopaedic on-call (or fracture clinic next day if uncomplicated)
    - If displaced or open fracture — immediate orthopaedic review
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
    - Admit only if: open fracture, compartment syndrome, displaced fracture requiring surgical fixation, or severe pain requiring IV analgesia
    - For admitted patients: maintain elevation, neurovascular observations 4-hourly
    - Arrange definitive imaging (MRI) within 48h if X-ray negative and high suspicion
    - Pre-operative workup if surgical fixation planned
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
    - **Fracture clinic follow-up within 1 week**
    - Cast for 6–8 weeks (non-displaced), then reassess with X-ray
    - Consider surgical fixation (Herbert screw) for displaced, proximal pole fractures, or delayed union
    - Long-term: hand therapy after cast removal for stiffness and grip strength
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
    - **Fluids:** NBM after midnight if surgery planned; otherwise free oral fluids
    - **VTE prophylaxis:** Not routine unless multiple trauma or risk factors (e.g. age >60, obesity, immobility). Consider TED stockings or LMWH per [local policy].
    - **Diet:** Normal unless pre-op NBM
    - **Observations:** Limb neurovascular obs 4-hourly (cap refill, sensation, motor function); baseline NEWS2
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line:**
    - Paracetamol 1g PO QDS (max 4g/day)
    - Ibuprofen 400mg PO TDS (max 1.2g/day) — use with caution in asthma, renal impairment, GI bleeding risk. *[Check local policy for dose in elderly]*
- **Common alternatives:**
    - Codeine phosphate 30–60mg PO QDS (if paracetamol/ibuprofen insufficient). Often combined with paracetamol (co-codamol 8/500–30/500). *[Check local policy for maximum dose]*
    - Oral morphine solution (if severe pain) — dose per [local policy]
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
    - NSAIDs contraindicated in active peptic ulcer, severe renal failure (eGFR <30), severe asthma
    - Codeine: avoid in patients with CYP2D6 ultra-rapid metabolisers (risk of toxicity) – not routinely tested, but caution in young children / breastfeeding? Not relevant here.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
    - NSAIDs: avoid if eGFR <30; reduce dose or use with caution if 30–60
    - Paracetamol: may need dose reduction in severe hepatic impairment (e.g. liver cirrhosis) — max 2–3g/day
- **Most dangerous dose error:** *Prescribing NSAIDs without checking renal function / GI risk.* In elderly or renal impairment, can cause acute kidney injury. Always check eGFR.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
    - Median nerve compression / acute carpal tunnel syndrome
    - Compartment syndrome (rare)
    - Infection (open fractures)
    - Cast-related: pressure sores, plaster burns
- **Chronic:**
    - **Avascular necrosis (AVN) of proximal pole:** highest risk if proximal pole fracture (non-union rate 30–50%). Result: chronic pain, osteoarthritis.
    - **Non-union:** most common complication overall. Risk factors: proximal pole, displacement, smoking.
    - **Osteoarthritis** (radiocarpal) — long-term after non-union or AVN
    - **Stiffness** (especially if prolonged immobilisation) — preventable with hand therapy
    - **Complex regional pain syndrome (CRPS) type 1** — after wrist trauma. Features: swelling, colour changes, pain out of proportion.
sections.Prognosis: - **Non-displaced waist fracture:** 90–95% union rate with 6–8 weeks cast immobilisation. Return to normal function 3–6 months. Proximal pole fractures have worse outcomes (40–60% union). Smokers: non-union risk 3–4 times higher.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always treat as scaphoid fracture if clinical suspicion + negative X-ray** — immobilise and arrange MRI within 2 weeks. Don’t dismiss unless X-ray clearly normal + no tenderness.
- **Document median nerve status** at presentation and every review — it’s your medicolegal safeguard.
- **Refer to hand therapy early** — after cast removal, stiffness is the main functional problem. A proactive referral prevents chronic disability.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not rely on X-ray alone** — up to 20% of scaphoid fractures are occult on initial radiographs.
- **Do not forget to check the thumb** — scaphoid fractures often missed because the thumb isn’t examined (scaphoid compression test, tubercle tenderness).
- **Do not prescribe NSAIDs in undifferentiated wrist pain without checking eGFR** — especially in elderly / diabetics.
- **Do not discharge without safety netting** — if sent home without cast and later found to have fracture, risk of non-union is increased.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
    - New or worsening numbness / tingling in thumb, index, middle finger (median nerve)
    - Severe pain unrelieved by simple analgesia, especially at rest or with passive finger extension
    - Cast becomes too tight, swollen, or wet/damaged
    - Fever, redness, spreading pain — possible infection
- **Follow-up:**
    - Fracture clinic within 1 week (or next available)
    - If MRI arranged: book MRI within 10–14 days; patient should continue cast/MCP splint until reviewed
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call orthopaedic registrar immediately if:**
    - Open fracture (any wound over scaphoid)
    - Displaced fracture on X-ray (step or gap >1 mm)
    - Signs of acute carpal tunnel syndrome (thenar weakness, severe paraesthesia)
    - Compartment syndrome suspicion (pain on passive stretch, tense compartment)
- **Escalate to anaesthetics/ICU if:**
    - NEWS2 ≥5 or any single parameter red (e.g. lactate >2 mmol/L, GCS <15 if no alternative cause)
    - Severe pain requiring IV opiates not controlled in ED

rawMarkdown:
# Scaphoid Fracture

**One-liner:** Common wrist fracture in young adults after fall on outstretched hand; high risk of avascular necrosis (AVN) and non-union if missed or undertreated.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Acute carpal tunnel syndrome:** Median nerve compression from fracture haematoma — *key finding: thenar weakness, loss of two-point discrimination (<6 mm) or severe paraesthesia*. Needs urgent surgical decompression.
- **Compartment syndrome of forearm/wrist:** Rare but catastrophic. *Key finding: pain on passive extension of fingers out of proportion to injury + tense swelling*. Check compartment pressure if suspected.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
    - Fall on outstretched hand (FOOSH) — typically young adult (15–40 y)
    - Radial-sided wrist pain, worse with grip and thumb movements
    - Snuffbox tenderness (anatomical snuffbox — *most sensitive sign*)
    - Tenderness over scaphoid tubercle (volar wrist, just distal to distal wrist crease)
    - Pain on axial loading of thumb (compression test)
    - Reduced range of wrist motion (especially extension)
- **Key exam findings:**
    - Swelling may be minimal — pain out of proportion to swelling
    - Pain on scaphoid compression: thumb metacarpal pushed proximally
    - No deformity in most cases (unless displaced)
    - Check distal neurovascular status: median, ulnar, radial nerves

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Scaphoid fracture (most likely) —** snuffbox tenderness, pain on axial compression
- **Distal radius fracture —** obvious deformity, radial styloid tenderness, dinner fork deformity (Colles’)
- **Wrist sprain / ligament injury —** no point tenderness over scaphoid, full motion less painful
- **De Quervain’s tenosynovitis —** Finkelstein test positive, no FOOSH history
- **Thumb metacarpal / trapezium fracture —** pain more ulnar or at base of thumb
- **Carpal instability (scapholunate dissociation) —** positive Watson’s test, often associated with history of hyperextension
- **Splitter:** *Exquisite tenderness precisely in anatomical snuffbox + pain on thumb axial compression = scaphoid until proven otherwise; if both absent and X-ray negative, think sprain*

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**
    - Clinical examination as above — snuffbox tenderness has sensitivity ~90% for acute fracture
    - Assess for median nerve function (thumb abduction, sensation to pulp of index)
    - Check radial pulse and capillary refill
- **Bloods:**
    - Typically not required unless planned for surgery (FBC, UAE, coagulation)
- **Imaging:**
    - **First-line:** Plain X-ray wrist — scaphoid view (PA in ulnar deviation) + lateral + oblique views. *If initial X-ray negative but clinical suspicion high → treat as fracture*.
    - **Second-line (if high suspicion after 10–14 days of negative X-ray):** MRI wrist (gold standard, sensitivity >99%) or CT if MRI unavailable. *Don’t delay treatment waiting for imaging.*
- **Special tests:**
    - **Scaphoid compression test** (axial load on thumb) — positive if pain at scaphoid
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**
    - No validated score in routine use; diagnosis is clinical + imaging
- **Never-miss test:** *MRI wrist within 10–14 days if initial X-ray negative and clinical suspicion high.* Missing an occult scaphoid fracture leads to AVN.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
    - Immobilise in scaphoid cast or below-elbow backslab (thumb immobilised — position: wrist neutral, thumb opposed). Simple volar wrist splint acceptable if cast unavailable.
    - Analgesia: paracetamol 1g PO QDS + ibuprofen 400mg PO TDS (if no contraindication)
    - Ice pack, elevation, simple sling
    - Refer to orthopaedic on-call (or fracture clinic next day if uncomplicated)
    - If displaced or open fracture — immediate orthopaedic review
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
    - Admit only if: open fracture, compartment syndrome, displaced fracture requiring surgical fixation, or severe pain requiring IV analgesia
    - For admitted patients: maintain elevation, neurovascular observations 4-hourly
    - Arrange definitive imaging (MRI) within 48h if X-ray negative and high suspicion
    - Pre-operative workup if surgical fixation planned
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
    - **Fracture clinic follow-up within 1 week**
    - Cast for 6–8 weeks (non-displaced), then reassess with X-ray
    - Consider surgical fixation (Herbert screw) for displaced, proximal pole fractures, or delayed union
    - Long-term: hand therapy after cast removal for stiffness and grip strength
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
    - **Fluids:** NBM after midnight if surgery planned; otherwise free oral fluids
    - **VTE prophylaxis:** Not routine unless multiple trauma or risk factors (e.g. age >60, obesity, immobility). Consider TED stockings or LMWH per [local policy].
    - **Diet:** Normal unless pre-op NBM
    - **Observations:** Limb neurovascular obs 4-hourly (cap refill, sensation, motor function); baseline NEWS2

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line:**
    - Paracetamol 1g PO QDS (max 4g/day)
    - Ibuprofen 400mg PO TDS (max 1.2g/day) — use with caution in asthma, renal impairment, GI bleeding risk. *[Check local policy for dose in elderly]*
- **Common alternatives:**
    - Codeine phosphate 30–60mg PO QDS (if paracetamol/ibuprofen insufficient). Often combined with paracetamol (co-codamol 8/500–30/500). *[Check local policy for maximum dose]*
    - Oral morphine solution (if severe pain) — dose per [local policy]
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
    - NSAIDs contraindicated in active peptic ulcer, severe renal failure (eGFR <30), severe asthma
    - Codeine: avoid in patients with CYP2D6 ultra-rapid metabolisers (risk of toxicity) – not routinely tested, but caution in young children / breastfeeding? Not relevant here.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
    - NSAIDs: avoid if eGFR <30; reduce dose or use with caution if 30–60
    - Paracetamol: may need dose reduction in severe hepatic impairment (e.g. liver cirrhosis) — max 2–3g/day
- **Most dangerous dose error:** *Prescribing NSAIDs without checking renal function / GI risk.* In elderly or renal impairment, can cause acute kidney injury. Always check eGFR.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
    - Median nerve compression / acute carpal tunnel syndrome
    - Compartment syndrome (rare)
    - Infection (open fractures)
    - Cast-related: pressure sores, plaster burns
- **Chronic:**
    - **Avascular necrosis (AVN) of proximal pole:** highest risk if proximal pole fracture (non-union rate 30–50%). Result: chronic pain, osteoarthritis.
    - **Non-union:** most common complication overall. Risk factors: proximal pole, displacement, smoking.
    - **Osteoarthritis** (radiocarpal) — long-term after non-union or AVN
    - **Stiffness** (especially if prolonged immobilisation) — preventable with hand therapy
    - **Complex regional pain syndrome (CRPS) type 1** — after wrist trauma. Features: swelling, colour changes, pain out of proportion.

### Prognosis
- **Non-displaced waist fracture:** 90–95% union rate with 6–8 weeks cast immobilisation. Return to normal function 3–6 months. Proximal pole fractures have worse outcomes (40–60% union). Smokers: non-union risk 3–4 times higher.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always treat as scaphoid fracture if clinical suspicion + negative X-ray** — immobilise and arrange MRI within 2 weeks. Don’t dismiss unless X-ray clearly normal + no tenderness.
- **Document median nerve status** at presentation and every review — it’s your medicolegal safeguard.
- **Refer to hand therapy early** — after cast removal, stiffness is the main functional problem. A proactive referral prevents chronic disability.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not rely on X-ray alone** — up to 20% of scaphoid fractures are occult on initial radiographs.
- **Do not forget to check the thumb** — scaphoid fractures often missed because the thumb isn’t examined (scaphoid compression test, tubercle tenderness).
- **Do not prescribe NSAIDs in undifferentiated wrist pain without checking eGFR** — especially in elderly / diabetics.
- **Do not discharge without safety netting** — if sent home without cast and later found to have fracture, risk of non-union is increased.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**
    - New or worsening numbness / tingling in thumb, index, middle finger (median nerve)
    - Severe pain unrelieved by simple analgesia, especially at rest or with passive finger extension
    - Cast becomes too tight, swollen, or wet/damaged
    - Fever, redness, spreading pain — possible infection
- **Follow-up:**
    - Fracture clinic within 1 week (or next available)
    - If MRI arranged: book MRI within 10–14 days; patient should continue cast/MCP splint until reviewed

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call orthopaedic registrar immediately if:**
    - Open fracture (any wound over scaphoid)
    - Displaced fracture on X-ray (step or gap >1 mm)
    - Signs of acute carpal tunnel syndrome (thenar weakness, severe paraesthesia)
    - Compartment syndrome suspicion (pain on passive stretch, tense compartment)
- **Escalate to anaesthetics/ICU if:**
    - NEWS2 ≥5 or any single parameter red (e.g. lactate >2 mmol/L, GCS <15 if no alternative cause)
    - Severe pain requiring IV opiates not controlled in ED
